(新字体; 'new character form'), by a process similar to [China](/wiki/China "China")'s [simplification efforts](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"), with the intention to increase [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") among the general public. Since the 1920s, the [Japanese government](/wiki/Japanese_government "Japanese government") has published character lists periodically to help direct the education of its citizenry through the myriad Chinese characters that exist. There are nearly 3,000 *kanji* [used in Japanese names](/wiki/Jinmeiy%C5%8D_kanji "Jinmeiyō kanji") and [in common communication](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Chinese characters used in Japanese writing

This article is about the Chinese-derived characters used in Japanese writing. For other uses, see [Kanji (disambiguation)](/wiki/Kanji_(disambiguation) "Kanji (disambiguation)").

***Kanji*** ([/ˈkændʒi, ˈkɑːn-/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[1]](#cite_note-1) Japanese: 漢字, [hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana"): かんじ, [Katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana"): カンジ, pronounced [[kaɲ.dʑi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-kanji.ogg "File:Ja-kanji.ogg"), '[Han](/wiki/Han_Chinese "Han Chinese") characters'[[2]](#cite_note-Daijisen-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Daijisen-Kan-3)) are [logographic](/wiki/Logographic "Logographic") [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters"), historically adapted from [Chinese writing scripts](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts "Chinese family of scripts"), used in the [writing](/wiki/Writing "Writing") of [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language").[[4]](#cite_note-4) They comprised a major part of the [Japanese writing system](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system") during the time of [Old Japanese](/wiki/Old_Japanese "Old Japanese") and are still used today, along with the subsequently derived [syllabic](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary") ([phonographic](/wiki/Phonogram_(linguistics) "Phonogram (linguistics)")) [scripts](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") of *[hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana")* and *[katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")*.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Most *Kanji* characters have two [pronunciations](/wiki/Pronunciation "Pronunciation"): *[kun'yomi](/wiki/Kun%27yomi "Kun'yomi")*, based on the sounds of [vernacular](/wiki/Vernacular "Vernacular") Japanese, where the *Kanji* is often [phonetically transcribed](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") with *[furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")*; and *[on'yomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi")*, based on the imitation of the original [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") sound when it was [borrowed](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese"). Some *Kanji* characters were indigenously invented in Japan by constructing character components derived from other Chinese characters.

After the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration"), Japan made its own efforts to simplify the *Kanji* characters, now known as **[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")** (新字体; 'new character form'), by a process similar to [China](/wiki/China "China")'s [simplification efforts](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"), with the intention to increase [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") among the general public. Since the 1920s, the [Japanese government](/wiki/Japanese_government "Japanese government") has published character lists periodically to help direct the education of its citizenry through the myriad Chinese characters that exist. There are nearly 3,000 *kanji* [used in Japanese names](/wiki/Jinmeiy%C5%8D_kanji "Jinmeiyō kanji") and [in common communication](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji").

The term *kanji* is a direct [borrowing](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") and phonetic reading (*[on'yomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi")*) of the Chinese word *[hanzi](/wiki/Hanzi "Hanzi")* ([traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 漢字; [simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 汉字; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): **hànzì**), which is one of the formal terms used when referring to Chinese characters.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The significant use of Chinese characters in Japan first began to take hold around the 5th century CE and has since had a profound influence in shaping Japanese culture, language, literature, history, and records.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyake20038-9) [Inkstone](/wiki/Inkstone "Inkstone") artifacts at archaeological sites dating back to the earlier [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") were also found to contain Chinese characters.[[10]](#cite_note-KT-10)

Although some characters, as used in Japanese and Chinese, have similar meanings and pronunciations, others have meanings or pronunciations that are unique to one language or the other. For example, [誠](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%AA%A0 "wikt:誠") means 'honest' in both languages but is pronounced *makoto* or *sei* in Japanese, and *chéng* in [Standard Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin_Chinese "Standard Mandarin Chinese"). Individual kanji characters and multi-kanji words invented in Japan from Chinese [morphemes](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme") have been borrowed into Chinese, Korean, and Vietnamese in recent times. These are known as [Wasei-kango](/wiki/Wasei-kango "Wasei-kango"), or Japanese-made Chinese words. For example, the word for [telephone](/wiki/Telephone "Telephone"), [電話](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%9B%BB%E8%A9%B1 "wikt:電話") *denwa* in Japanese, was derived from the Chinese words for "electric" and "conversation." It was then [calqued](/wiki/Calque "Calque") as *diànhuà* in Mandarin Chinese, *điện thoại* in Vietnamese and 전화 *jeonhwa* in Korean.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Nihonshoki_tanaka_version.jpg/330px-Nihonshoki_tanaka_version.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nihonshoki_tanaka_version.jpg)

*[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")* (720 CE), considered by historians and archaeologists as the most complete extant historical record of ancient Japan, was written entirely in kanji.

[Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_character "Chinese character") first came to [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") on official seals, letters, swords, coins, mirrors, and other decorative items imported from [China](/wiki/History_of_China "History of China").[[12]](#cite_note-12) The earliest known instance of such an import was the [King of Na gold seal](/wiki/King_of_Na_gold_seal "King of Na gold seal") given by [Emperor Guangwu of Han](/wiki/Emperor_Guangwu_of_Han "Emperor Guangwu of Han") to a [Wa](/wiki/Wa_(name_of_Japan) "Wa (name of Japan)") emissary in 57 CE.[[13]](#cite_note-Ki-13) Chinese coins as well as [inkstones](/wiki/Inkstone "Inkstone") from the first century CE have also been found in [Yayoi period](/wiki/Yayoi_period "Yayoi period") archaeological sites.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyake20038-9)[[10]](#cite_note-KT-10) However, the Japanese people of that era probably had little to no comprehension of the script, and they would remain relatively illiterate until the fifth century CE, when writing in Japan became more widespread.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyake20038-9) According to the *[Nihon Shoki](/wiki/Nihon_Shoki "Nihon Shoki")* and *[Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki")*, a semi-legendary scholar called [Wani](/wiki/Wani_(scholar) "Wani (scholar)") was dispatched to Japan by the [Kingdom of Baekje](/wiki/Baekje "Baekje") during the reign of [Emperor Ōjin](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cjin "Emperor Ōjin") in the early fifth century, bringing with him knowledge of [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") and Chinese characters.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyake20039-14)

The earliest Japanese documents were probably written by bilingual Chinese or Korean officials employed at the [Yamato](/wiki/Yamato_period "Yamato period") court.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyake20038-9) For example, the diplomatic correspondence from [King Bu of Wa](/wiki/Five_kings_of_Wa "Five kings of Wa") to [Emperor Shun of Liu Song](/wiki/Emperor_Shun_of_Liu_Song "Emperor Shun of Liu Song") in 478 CE has been praised for its skillful use of [allusion](/wiki/Allusion "Allusion"). Later, groups of people called *fuhito* were organized under the monarch to read and write [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese"). During the reign of [Empress Suiko](/wiki/Empress_Suiko "Empress Suiko") (593–628), the Yamato court began sending full-scale diplomatic missions to China, which resulted in a large increase in Chinese literacy at the Japanese court.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMiyake20039-14)

In ancient times, paper was so rare that people wrote kanji onto thin, rectangular strips of wood, called *[mokkan](/wiki/Mokkan "Mokkan")* ([木簡](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9C%A8%E7%B0%A1#Japanese "wikt:木簡")). These wooden boards were used for communication between government offices, tags for goods transported between various countries, and the practice of writing. The oldest written kanji in Japan discovered so far were written in ink on wood as a wooden strip dated to the 7th century, a record of trading for cloth and salt.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The Japanese language had no written form at the time Chinese characters were introduced, and texts were written and read only in Chinese. Later, during the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794–1185), a system known as *[kanbun](/wiki/Kanbun "Kanbun")* emerged, which involved using Chinese text with [diacritical marks](/wiki/Diacritical_mark "Diacritical mark") to allow Japanese speakers to read Chinese sentences and restructure them into Japanese on the fly, by changing word order and adding particles and verb endings, in accordance with the rules of [Japanese grammar](/wiki/Japanese_grammar "Japanese grammar"). This was essentially a kind of codified [sight translation](/wiki/Translation "Translation").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Chinese characters also came to be used to write texts in the vernacular [Japanese language](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), resulting in the modern *[kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")* syllabaries. Around 650 CE, a writing system called *[man'yōgana](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dgana "Man'yōgana")* (used in the ancient poetry [anthology](/wiki/Anthology "Anthology") *[Man'yōshū](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Man'yōshū")*) evolved that used a number of Chinese characters for their sound, rather than for their meaning. *Man'yōgana* written in [cursive style](/wiki/Grass_script "Grass script") evolved into *[hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana")* (literally "fluttering *kana*" in reference to the motion of the brush during cursive writing), or *onna-de*, that is, "ladies' hand",[[16]](#cite_note-16) a writing system that was accessible to women (who were denied [higher education](/wiki/Higher_education "Higher education")). Major works of [Heian-era](/wiki/Heian-era "Heian-era") [literature](/wiki/Literature "Literature") by women were written in *hiragana*. *[Katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")* (literally "partial *kana*", in reference to the practice of using a part of a kanji character) emerged via a parallel path: [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") students simplified *man'yōgana* to a single constituent element. Thus the two other writing systems, *hiragana* and *katakana*, referred to collectively as *kana*, are descended from kanji. In contrast with *kana* ([仮名](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%AE%E5%90%8D#Japanese "wikt:仮名"), literally "borrowed name", in reference to the character being "borrowed" as a label for its sound), kanji are also called *mana* ([真名](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%9C%9F%E5%90%8D#Japanese "wikt:真名"), literally "true name", in reference to the character being used as a label for its meaning).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In modern Japanese, kanji are used to write certain words or parts of words (usually [content words](/wiki/Content_word "Content word") such as [nouns](/wiki/Noun "Noun"), [adjective](/wiki/Adjective "Adjective") [stems](/wiki/Word_stem "Word stem"), and [verb](/wiki/Verb "Verb") [stems](/wiki/Word_stem "Word stem")), while *hiragana* are used to write [inflected](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection") verb and adjective endings, [phonetic complements](/wiki/Phonetic_complement "Phonetic complement") to disambiguate readings (*[okurigana](/wiki/Okurigana "Okurigana")*), [particles](/wiki/Grammatical_particle "Grammatical particle"), and miscellaneous words which have no kanji or whose kanji are considered obscure or too difficult to read or remember. *[Katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")* are mostly used for representing [onomatopoeia](/wiki/Onomatopoeia "Onomatopoeia"), [non-Japanese loanwords](/wiki/Gairaigo "Gairaigo") (except those borrowed from [ancient Chinese](/wiki/Old_Chinese "Old Chinese")), the names of plants and animals (with exceptions), and for emphasis on certain words.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Orthographic reform and lists of kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Orthographic reform and lists of kanji")]

Main article: [Japanese script reform](/wiki/Japanese_script_reform "Japanese script reform")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Chikanobu_Shin_Bijin_No._20.jpg/250px-Y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Chikanobu_Shin_Bijin_No._20.jpg)](/wiki/File:Y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Chikanobu_Shin_Bijin_No._20.jpg)

A young woman practicing kanji. [Ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") woodblock print by [Yōshū Chikanobu](/wiki/Toyohara_Chikanobu "Toyohara Chikanobu"), 1897.

Since ancient times, there has been a strong opinion in Japan that kanji is the orthodox form of writing, but others have argued against it.[[17]](#cite_note-17) [Kamo no Mabuchi](/wiki/Kamo_no_Mabuchi "Kamo no Mabuchi"), a scholar of the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"), criticized the large number of characters in kanji. He also appreciated the small number of characters in *kana* characters and argued for the limitation of kanji.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After the [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") and as Japan entered an era of active exchange with foreign countries, the need for script reform in Japan began to be called for. Some scholars argued for the abolition of kanji and the writing of Japanese using only *kana* or Latin characters. These views were not widespread.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

However, the need to limit the number of kanji characters was understood, and in May 1923, the Japanese government announced 1,962 kanji characters for regular use. In 1940, the [Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") decided on the "Table of Restricted Kanji for Weapons Names" (兵器名称用制限漢字表, *heiki meishō yō seigen kanji hyō*) which limited the number of kanji that could be used for weapons names to 1,235. In 1942, the National Language Council announced the "Standard Kanji Table" (標準漢字表, *hyōjun kanji-hyō*) with a total of 2,528 characters, showing the standard for kanji used by ministries and agencies and in general society.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1946, after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and under the [Allied occupation of Japan](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan"), the Japanese government, guided by the [Supreme Commander of the Allied Powers](/wiki/Supreme_Commander_of_the_Allied_Powers "Supreme Commander of the Allied Powers"), instituted a series of [orthographic](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") reforms, to help children learn and to simplify kanji use in literature and periodicals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The number of characters in circulation was reduced, and formal lists of characters to be learned during each grade of school were established.
Some characters were given simplified [glyphs](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph"), called *[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")* (新字体). Many variant forms of characters and obscure alternatives for common characters were officially discouraged.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

These are simply guidelines, so many characters outside these standards are still widely known and commonly used; these are known as *[hyōgaiji](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgaiji "Hyōgaiji")* (表外字).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### *Kyōiku* kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Kyōiku kanji")]

Main article: [Kyōiku kanji](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Diku_kanji "Kyōiku kanji")

The *kyōiku kanji* (教育漢字; lit. "education kanji") are the 1,026 first kanji characters that Japanese children learn in elementary school, from first grade to sixth grade. The grade-level breakdown is known as the *gakunen-betsu kanji haitōhyō* ([学年別漢字配当表](/wiki/%E5%AD%A6%E5%B9%B4%E5%88%A5%E6%BC%A2%E5%AD%97%E9%85%8D%E5%BD%93%E8%A1%A8 "学年別漢字配当表")), or the *gakushū kanji* (学習漢字). This list of kanji is maintained by the [Japanese Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education,_Culture,_Sports,_Science_and_Technology_(Japan) "Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (Japan)") and prescribes which kanji characters and which kanji readings students should learn for each grade.

### *Jōyō* kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Jōyō kanji")]

Main article: [Jōyō kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji")

The *jōyō kanji* (常用漢字; regular-use kanji) are 2,136 characters consisting of all the *kyōiku* kanji, plus 1,110 additional kanji taught in junior high and high school.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In publishing, characters outside this category are often given *[furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")*. The *jōyō* kanji were introduced in 1981, replacing an older list of 1,850 characters known as the *[tōyō kanji](/wiki/T%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Tōyō kanji")* (当用漢字; general-use kanji), introduced in 1946. Originally numbering 1,945 characters, the *jōyō* kanji list was expanded to 2,136 in 2010. Some of the new characters were previously *jinmeiyō* kanji; some are used to write prefecture names: 阪, 熊, 奈, 岡, 鹿, 梨, 阜, 埼, 茨, 栃 and 媛.

### *Jinmeiyō* kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Jinmeiyō kanji")]

Main article: [Jinmeiyō kanji](/wiki/Jinmeiy%C5%8D_kanji "Jinmeiyō kanji")

As of September 25, 2017, the *jinmeiyō kanji* (人名用漢字; kanji for use in personal names) consists of 863 characters. Kanji on this list are mostly used in people's names and some are traditional variants of *jōyō* kanji. There were only 92 kanji in the original list published in 1952, but new additions have been made frequently. Sometimes the term *jinmeiyō* kanji refers to all 2,999 kanji from both the *jōyō* and *jinmeiyō* lists combined.

### *Hyōgai* kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hyōgai kanji")]

Main article: [Hyōgai kanji](/wiki/Hy%C5%8Dgai_kanji "Hyōgai kanji")

*Hyōgai kanji* (表外漢字; "unlisted characters") are any kanji not contained in the *jōyō* kanji and *jinmeiyō* kanji lists. These are generally written using traditional characters, but [extended *shinjitai*](/wiki/Extended_shinjitai "Extended shinjitai") forms exist.

### Japanese Industrial Standards for kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Japanese Industrial Standards for kanji")]

The [Japanese Industrial Standards](/wiki/Japanese_Industrial_Standards "Japanese Industrial Standards") for kanji and *kana* define character code-points for each kanji and *kana*, as well as other forms of writing such as the [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet"), [Cyrillic script](/wiki/Cyrillic_script "Cyrillic script"), [Greek alphabet](/wiki/Greek_alphabet "Greek alphabet"), [Arabic numerals](/wiki/Arabic_numerals "Arabic numerals"), etc. for use in information processing. They have had numerous revisions. The current standards are:

* [JIS X 0208](/wiki/JIS_X_0208 "JIS X 0208"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) the most recent version of the main standard. It has 6,355 kanji.
* [JIS X 0212](/wiki/JIS_X_0212 "JIS X 0212"),[[22]](#cite_note-22) a supplementary standard containing a further 5,801 kanji. This standard is rarely used, mainly because the common [Shift JIS](/wiki/Shift_JIS "Shift JIS") encoding system could not use it. This standard is effectively obsolete.
* [JIS X 0213](/wiki/JIS_X_0213 "JIS X 0213"),[[23]](#cite_note-23) a further revision which extended the JIS X 0208 set with 3,695 additional kanji, of which 2,743 (all but 952) were in JIS X 0212. The standard is in part designed to be compatible with Shift JIS encoding.
* JIS X 0221:1995, the Japanese version of the ISO 10646/[Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") standard.

#### Gaiji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Gaiji")]

"Gaiji" redirects here; not to be confused with [gaijin](/wiki/Gaijin "Gaijin").

*Gaiji* (外字; literally "external characters") are kanji that are not represented in existing Japanese [encoding systems](/wiki/Character_encoding "Character encoding"). These include variant forms of common kanji that need to be represented alongside the more conventional [glyph](/wiki/Glyph "Glyph") in reference works and can include non-kanji symbols as well.

*Gaiji* can be either user-defined characters, system-specific characters or third-party add-on products.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Both are a problem for information interchange, as the [code point](/wiki/Code_point "Code point") used to represent an external character will not be consistent from one computer or operating system to another.

*Gaiji* were nominally prohibited in JIS X 0208-1997 where the available number of code-points was reduced to only 940.[[25]](#cite_note-25) JIS X 0213-2000 used the entire range of code-points previously allocated to *gaiji*, making them completely unusable. Most desktop and mobile systems have moved to [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") negating the need for *gaiji* for most users. Historically, *gaiji* were used by Japanese mobile service providers for [emoji](/wiki/Emoji "Emoji").

[Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode") allows for optional encoding of *gaiji* in [private use areas](/wiki/Private_Use_(Unicode) "Private Use (Unicode)"), while [Adobe's SING](/wiki/OpenType#SING_gaiji_solution "OpenType") (Smart INdependent Glyphlets)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) technology allows the creation of customized gaiji.

The [Text Encoding Initiative](/wiki/Text_Encoding_Initiative "Text Encoding Initiative") uses a ⟨g⟩ element to encode any non-standard character or glyph, including gaiji. The *g* stands for *gaiji*.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Total number of kanji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Total number of kanji")]

There is no definitive count of kanji characters, just as there is none of Chinese characters generally. The *[Dai Kan-Wa Jiten](/wiki/Dai_Kan-Wa_Jiten "Dai Kan-Wa Jiten")*, which is considered to be comprehensive in Japan, contains about 50,000 characters. The *[Zhonghua Zihai](/wiki/Zhonghua_Zihai "Zhonghua Zihai")*, published in 1994 in China, contains about 85,000 characters, but the majority of them are not in common use in any country, and many are obscure variants or archaic forms.[[30]](#cite_note-taipei-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

A list of 2,136 [*jōyō* kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji") is regarded as necessary for functional literacy in Japanese. Approximately a thousand more characters are commonly used and readily understood by the majority in Japan and a few thousand more find occasional use, particularly in specialized fields of study but those may be obscure to most out of context. A total of 13,108 characters can be encoded in various [Japanese Industrial Standards for kanji](#Japanese_Industrial_Standards_for_kanji).

## Readings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Readings")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section may **require [cleanup](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")** to meet Wikipedia's [quality standards](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style "Wikipedia:Manual of Style"). The specific problem is: **Overly verbose 'readings' section.** Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Kanji "Special:EditPage/Kanji") if you can. *(March 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Individual kanji may be used to write one or more different words or [morphemes](/wiki/Morphemes "Morphemes"), leading to different pronunciations or "readings." The correct reading may be determined by contextual cues (such as whether the character represents part of a compound word versus an independent word), the exact intended meaning of the word, and its position within the sentence. For example, 今日 is mostly read *kyō*, meaning "today", but in formal writing it is read *konnichi*, meaning "nowadays". *[Furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")* is used to specify ambiguous readings, such as rare, literary, or otherwise non-standard readings.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Readings are categorized as either kun'yomi (訓読み) (literally "meaning reading"), native Japanese, or on'yomi (音読み) (literally "sound reading"), borrowed from Chinese. Most kanji have at least a single reading of each category, though some have only one, such as *kiku* (菊; "chrysanthemum", an *on*-reading) or *iwashi* (鰯; "sardine", a *kun*-reading); Japanese-coined kanji (*kokuji*) often only have *kun'yomi* readings.

Some common kanji have ten or more possible readings; the most complex common example is [生](/wiki/Okurigana#生 "Okurigana"), which is read as *sei*, *shō*, *nama*, *ki*, *o-u*, *i-kiru*, *i-kasu*, *i-keru*, *u-mu*, *u-mareru*, *ha-eru*, and *ha-yasu*, totaling eight basic readings (the first two are *on*, while the rest are *kun*), or 12 if related verbs are counted as distinct.

### *On'yomi* (Sino-Japanese reading)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)")]

Main article: [On'yomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi")

The ***on'yomi*** (音読み; [[oɰ̃jomi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese"), lit. "sound(-based) reading"), the [Sino-Japanese](/wiki/Sino-Japanese_vocabulary "Sino-Japanese vocabulary") reading, is the modern descendant of the Japanese approximation of the base Chinese pronunciation of the character at the time it was introduced. It was often previously referred to as **translation reading**, as it was recreated readings of the Chinese pronunciation but was not the Chinese pronunciation or reading itself, similar to the English pronunciation of Latin loanwords. There also exist kanji created by the Japanese and given an *on'yomi* reading despite not being a Chinese-derived or a Chinese-originating character. Some kanji were introduced from different parts of China at different times, and so have multiple *on'yomi*, and often multiple meanings. Kanji invented in Japan (*[kokuji](/wiki/Kokuji "Kokuji")*) would not normally be expected to have *on'yomi*, but there are exceptions, such as the character 働 "to work", which has the *kun'yomi* "*hatara(ku)*" and the *on'yomi* "*dō*", and 腺 "gland", which has only the *on'yomi* "*sen*"—in both cases these come from the *on'yomi* of the phonetic component, respectively 動 "*dō*" and 泉 "*sen*".

### *Kun'yomi* (native reading)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Kun'yomi (native reading)")]

Main article: [Kun'yomi](/wiki/Kun%27yomi "Kun'yomi")

The ***kun'yomi*** (訓読み; [[kɯɰ̃jomi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese"), lit. "meaning reading"), the native reading, is a reading based on the pronunciation of a native [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language") word, or *[yamato kotoba](/wiki/Yamato_kotoba "Yamato kotoba")*, that closely approximated the meaning of the [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") character when it was introduced. As with *on'yomi*, there can be multiple *kun'yomi* for the same kanji, and some kanji have no *kun'yomi* at all.

### *Ateji*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ateji")]

Main article: [Ateji](/wiki/Ateji "Ateji")

***Ateji*** (当て字) are characters used only for their sounds. In this case, pronunciation is still based on a standard reading, or used only for meaning (broadly a form of *ateji*, narrowly *jukujikun*). Therefore, only the full compound—not the individual character—has a reading. There are also [special cases](#Special_readings) where the reading is completely different, often based on a historical or traditional reading.

The analogous phenomenon occurs to a much lesser degree in [Chinese varieties](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese"), where there are [literary and colloquial readings of Chinese characters](/wiki/Literary_and_colloquial_readings_of_Chinese_characters "Literary and colloquial readings of Chinese characters")—borrowed readings and native readings. In Chinese these borrowed readings and native readings are etymologically related, since they are between Chinese varieties (which are related), not from Chinese to Japanese (which are not related). They thus form [doublets](/wiki/Doublet_(linguistics) "Doublet (linguistics)") and are generally similar, analogous to different *on'yomi*, reflecting different stages of Chinese borrowings into Japanese.

### Gairaigo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Gairaigo")]

Longer readings exist for non-*Jōyō* characters and non-kanji symbols, where a long [gairaigo](/wiki/Gairaigo "Gairaigo") word may be the reading (this is classed as *kun'yomi*—see [single character gairaigo](#Single_character_gairaigo), below)—the character 糎 has the seven *kana* reading センチメートル *senchimētoru* "centimeter", though it is generally written as "cm" (with two half-width characters, so occupying one space); another common example is '%' (the percent sign), which has the five kana reading パーセント *pāsento*.

### Mixed readings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Mixed readings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/J%C5%ABbako.jpg/250px-J%C5%ABbako.jpg)](/wiki/File:J%C5%ABbako.jpg)

A *jūbako* (重箱), which has a mixed *on-kun* reading

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Pail%2Cyutou%2Csoba-yu%2Ckatori-city%2Cjapan.JPG/250px-Pail%2Cyutou%2Csoba-yu%2Ckatori-city%2Cjapan.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pail,yutou,soba-yu,katori-city,japan.JPG)

A *yutō* (湯桶), which has a mixed *kun-on* reading

There are many kanji compounds that use a mixture of *on'yomi* and *kun'yomi*; these may be considered [hybrid words](/wiki/Hybrid_word "Hybrid word"). Readings in which the first kanji is *on'yomi* and the second is *kun'yomi* are classified as *jūbakoyomi* (重箱読み; multi-layered food box reading), while *kun-on* words are classified as *yutōyomi* (湯桶読み; hot liquid pail reading). The words *jūbako* and *yutō* are themselves examples of the reading patterns they represent (they are [autological words](/wiki/Autological_word "Autological word")). Other examples include *basho* (場所; "place", *kun-on*), *kin'iro* (金色; "golden", *on-kun*) and *aikidō* (合気道; the martial art [Aikido](/wiki/Aikido "Aikido")", *kun-on-on*).

*[Ateji](/wiki/Ateji "Ateji")* often use mixed readings. For instance, the city of [Sapporo](/wiki/Sapporo "Sapporo") (サッポロ), whose name derives from the [Ainu language](/wiki/Ainu_language "Ainu language") and has no meaning in Japanese, is written with the *on-kun* compound 札幌 (*sapporo*) (which includes *[sokuon](/wiki/Sokuon "Sokuon")* as if it were a purely *on* compound).

### Special readings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Special readings")]

*Gikun* ([義訓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%BE%A9%E8%A8%93#Japanese "wikt:義訓")) and *jukujikun* ([熟字訓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%86%9F%E5%AD%97%E8%A8%93#Japanese "wikt:熟字訓")) are readings of kanji combinations that have no direct correspondence to the characters' individual *on'yomi* or *kun'yomi*. From the point of view of the character, rather than the word, this is known as a *nankun* ([難訓](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%9B%A3%E8%A8%93#Japanese "wikt:難訓"); "difficult reading"), and these are listed in kanji dictionaries under the entry for the character.

*Gikun* are other readings assigned to a character instead of its standard readings. An example is reading [寒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AF%92#Japanese "wikt:寒") (meaning "cold") as *fuyu* ("winter") rather than the standard readings *samu* or *kan*, and instead of the usual spelling for *fuyu* of [冬](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%86%AC#Japanese "wikt:冬"). Another example is using [煙草](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%85%99%E8%8D%89#Japanese "wikt:煙草") (lit. 'smoke grass') with the reading *tabako* ("tobacco") rather than the otherwise-expected readings of *\*kemuri-gusa* or *\*ensō*. Some of these, such as for *tabako*, have become [lexicalized](/wiki/Lexicalized "Lexicalized"), but in many cases this kind of use is typically non-standard and employed in specific contexts by individual writers. Aided with *[furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")*, *gikun* could be used to convey complex literary or poetic effect (especially if the readings contradict the kanji), or clarification if the referent may not be obvious.

*Jukujikun* are when the standard kanji for a word are related to the meaning, but not the sound. The word is pronounced as a whole, not corresponding to sounds of individual kanji. For example, [今朝](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%8A%E6%9C%9D#Japanese "wikt:今朝") ("this morning") is *jukujikun*. This word is not read as *\*ima'asa*, the expected *kun'yomi* of the characters, and only infrequently as *konchō*, the *on'yomi* of the characters. The most common reading is *kesa*, a native bisyllabic Japanese word that may be seen as a single [morpheme](/wiki/Morpheme "Morpheme"), or as a compound of *ke* (“this”, as in *kefu*, the older reading for [今日](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%8A%E6%97%A5#Japanese "wikt:今日"), “today”), and *asa*, “morning”.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Likewise, [今日](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%8A%E6%97%A5#Japanese "wikt:今日") ("today") is also *jukujikun*, usually read with the native reading *kyō*; its *on'yomi*, *konnichi*, does occur in certain words and expressions, especially in the broader sense "nowadays" or "current", such as [今日的](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%8A%E6%97%A5%E7%9A%84#Japanese "wikt:今日的") ("present-day"), although in the phrase *konnichi wa* ("good day"), *konnichi* is typically spelled wholly with *hiragana* rather than with the kanji 今日.

*Jukujikun* are primarily used for some native Japanese words, such as [Yamato](/wiki/Yamato_(disambiguation) "Yamato (disambiguation)") [大和](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E5%92%8C#Japanese "wikt:大和") or [倭](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%80%AD#Japanese "wikt:倭"), the name of the dominant ethnic group of Japan, a former Japanese province as well as ancient name for Japan), and for some old borrowings, such as [柳葉魚](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9F%B3%E8%91%89%E9%AD%9A#Japanese "wikt:柳葉魚") (*[shishamo](/wiki/Shishamo "Shishamo")*, literally "willow leaf fish") from Ainu, [煙草](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%85%99%E8%8D%89#Japanese "wikt:煙草") (*tabako*, literally “smoke grass”) from Portuguese, or [麦酒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%BA%A6%E9%85%92#Japanese "wikt:麦酒") (*bīru*, literally “wheat alcohol”) from Dutch, especially if the word was borrowed before the [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period"). Words whose kanji are *jukujikun* are often usually written as *hiragana* (if native), or *katakana* (if borrowed); some old borrowed words are also written as *hiragana*, especially Portuguese loanwords such as [かるた](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%81%8B%E3%82%8B%E3%81%9F#Japanese "wikt:かるた") (*karuta*) from Portuguese "*[carta](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/carta#Portuguese "wikt:carta")*" (English "card") or [てんぷら](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%81%A6%E3%82%93%E3%81%B7%E3%82%89#Japanese "wikt:てんぷら") (*tempura*) from Portuguese "*[tempora](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/tempora#Portuguese "wikt:tempora")*" (English “times, season”),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] as well as [たばこ](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%81%9F%E3%81%B0%E3%81%93#Japanese "wikt:たばこ") (*tabako*).

A case where *jukujikun* is used for [Sino-Japanese](/wiki/Sino-Japanese_vocabulary "Sino-Japanese vocabulary") is the word *kyōdai*, which, prototypically, means "brothers" and is spelt 兄(きょう)弟(だい) ("big and little brothers"). However, the meaning has been expanded to "siblings" in general, and can assume such spellings as 姉(きょう)妹(だい) ("(big and little) sisters", alternatively pronounced *shimai*), 兄(きょう)妹(だい) ("big brother and little sister") and 姉(きょう)弟(だい) ("big sister and little brother"). It is also possible to say *otoko kyōdai* ("male siblings; brothers") and *onna kyōdai* ("female siblings; sisters").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Sometimes, *jukujikun* can even have more kanji than there are syllables, examples being *kera* ([啄木鳥](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%95%84%E6%9C%A8%E9%B3%A5#Japanese "wikt:啄木鳥"), “woodpecker”), *gumi* ([胡頽子](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%83%A1%E9%A0%BD%E5%AD%90#Japanese "wikt:胡頽子"), “silver berry, oleaster”),[[36]](#cite_note-36) and *Hozumi* ([八月朔日](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%85%AB%E6%9C%88%E6%9C%94%E6%97%A5#Japanese "wikt:八月朔日"), a surname).[[37]](#cite_note-How_do_Japanese_names_work-37) This phenomenon is observed in animal names that are shortened and used as suffixes for zoological compound names, for example when [黄金虫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%BB%84%E9%87%91%E8%99%AB#Japanese "wikt:黄金虫"), normally read as *koganemushi*, is shortened to *kogane* in [黒黄金虫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%BB%92%E9%BB%84%E9%87%91%E8%99%AB#Japanese "wikt:黒黄金虫") *kurokogane*, although zoological names are commonly spelled with katakana rather than with kanji. Outside zoology, this type of shortening only occurs on a handful of words, for example [大元帥](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7%E5%85%83%E5%B8%A5#Japanese "wikt:大元帥") *daigen(sui)*, or the historical male name suffix [右衛門](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%B3%E8%A1%9B%E9%96%80#Japanese "wikt:右衛門") *-emon*, which was shortened from the word *uemon*.

The kanji compound for *jukujikun* is often idiosyncratic and created for the word, and there is no corresponding Chinese word with that spelling. In other cases, a kanji compound for an existing Chinese word is reused, where the Chinese word and *on'yomi* may or may not be used in Japanese. For example, [馴鹿](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%A6%B4%E9%B9%BF#Japanese "wikt:馴鹿") ("reindeer") is *jukujikun* for *tonakai*, from Ainu, but the *on'yomi* reading of *junroku* is also used. In some cases, Japanese coinages have subsequently been [borrowed back into Chinese](/wiki/Wasei-kango "Wasei-kango"), such as [鮟鱇](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%AE%9F%E9%B1%87#Japanese "wikt:鮟鱇") (*ankō*, "[monkfish](/wiki/Monkfish "Monkfish")").

The underlying word for *jukujikun* is a native Japanese word or foreign borrowing, which either does not have an existing kanji spelling (either *kun'yomi* or *ateji*) or for which a new kanji spelling is produced. Most often the word is a noun, which may be a simple noun (not a compound or derived from a verb), or may be a verb form or a fusional pronunciation. For example, the word [相撲](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%9B%B8%E6%92%B2#Japanese "wikt:相撲") (*sumō*, "[sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo")") is originally from the verb [争う](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%89%E3%81%86#Japanese "wikt:争う") (*sumau*, “to vie, to compete”), while [今日](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BB%8A%E6%97%A5#Japanese "wikt:今日") (*kyō*, "today") is fusional (from older *ke*, "this" + *fu*, "day").

In rare cases, *jukujikun* is also applied to inflectional words (verbs and adjectives), in which case there is frequently a corresponding Chinese word. The most common example of an inflectional *jukujikun* is the adjective [可愛い](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%AF%E6%84%9B%E3%81%84#Japanese "wikt:可愛い") (*kawai-i*, “cute”), originally *kawafayu-i*; the word ([可愛](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8F%AF%E6%84%9B "wikt:可愛")) is used in [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), but the corresponding *on'yomi* is not used in Japanese. By contrast, "appropriate" can be either [相応しい](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%9B%B8%E5%BF%9C%E3%81%97%E3%81%84#Japanese "wikt:相応しい") (*fusawa-shii*, as *jukujikun*) or [相応](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%9B%B8%E5%BF%9C#Japanese "wikt:相応") (*sōō*, as *on'yomi*). Which reading to use can be discerned by the presence or absence of the *-shii* ending (*[okurigana](/wiki/Okurigana "Okurigana")*). A common example of a verb with *jukujikun* is [流行る](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B5%81%E8%A1%8C%E3%82%8B#Japanese "wikt:流行る") (*haya-ru*, “to spread, to be in vogue”), corresponding to *on'yomi* [流行](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B5%81%E8%A1%8C#Japanese "wikt:流行") (*ryūkō*). A sample *jukujikun* deverbal (noun derived from a verb form) is [強請](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%B7%E8%AB%8B#Japanese "wikt:強請") (*yusuri*, "extortion"), from [強請る](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%B7%E8%AB%8B%E3%82%8B#Japanese "wikt:強請る") (*yusu-ru*, “to extort”), spelling from [強請](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BC%B7%E8%AB%8B#Japanese "wikt:強請") (*kyōsei*, "extortion"). Note that there are also compound verbs and, less commonly, compound adjectives, and while these may have multiple kanji without intervening characters, they are read using the usual *kun'yomi*. Examples include [面白い](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%9D%A2%E7%99%BD%E3%81%84#Japanese "wikt:面白い") (*omo-shiro-i*, "interesting", literally "face + white") and [狡賢い](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%8B%A1%E8%B3%A2%E3%81%84#Japanese "wikt:狡賢い") (*zuru-gashiko-i*, "sly", lit. "cunning, crafty + clever, smart").

Typographically, the *[furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")* for *jukujikun* are often written so they are centered across the entire word, or for inflectional words over the entire root—corresponding to the reading being related to the entire word—rather than each part of the word being centered over its corresponding character, as is often done for the usual phono-semantic readings.

### Single character gairaigo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Single character gairaigo")]

In some rare cases, kanji may have a reading borrowed from a modern foreign language (*[gairaigo](/wiki/Gairaigo "Gairaigo")*), though usually *gairaigo* are written in *katakana*. Notable examples include *pēji* (頁、ページ; page), *botan* (釦／鈕、ボタン; button), *zero* (零、ゼロ; zero), and *mētoru* (米、メートル; meter). These are classed as *kun'yomi*, because the character is used for its meaning—the *kun'yomi* label may sometimes be misleading, since most *kun'yomi* are native Japanese readings. The readings are also rendered in *katakana*, unlike the usual *hiragana* for native *kun'yomi*. Note that most of these characters are for units, particularly [SI units](/wiki/SI_units "SI units"), in many cases using new characters (*[kokuji](/wiki/Kokuji "Kokuji")*) coined during the [Meiji period](/wiki/Meiji_period "Meiji period"), such as *kiromētoru* (粁、キロメートル; kilometer, 米 "meter" + 千 "thousand").

### *Nanori*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Nanori")]

Main article: [Nanori](/wiki/Nanori "Nanori")

Some kanji also have lesser-known readings called *[nanori](/wiki/Nanori "Nanori")* (名乗り), which are mostly used for names (often [given names](/wiki/Given_name "Given name")) and, in general, are closely related to the *kun'yomi*. Place names sometimes also use *nanori* or, occasionally, unique readings not found elsewhere.

### When to use which reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: When to use which reading")]

Although there are general rules for when to use *on'yomi* and when to use *kun'yomi*, many kanji have multiple on- or kun-readings, and the language is littered with exceptions; how a character was meant to be read is sometimes ambiguous even to native speakers (this is especially true for names, both of people and places).

A single kanji followed by *okurigana* (*hiragana* forming part of a word)—such as the inflectable suffixes forming native verbs and adjectives like 赤い (*akai*; red) and 見る (*miru*; to see)—*always* indicates *kun'yomi*. *Okurigana* can indicate which *kun'yomi* to use, as in 食べる (*ta-beru*) versus 食う (*ku-u*), both meaning "(to) eat", but this is not always sufficient, as in 開く, which may be read as *a-ku* or *hira-ku*, both meaning "(to) open".

Kanji compounds (*jukuji*), especially [yojijukugo](/wiki/Yojijukugo "Yojijukugo"), usually, but not always, use *on'yomi*, usually (but not always) *kan-on*. In *ge-doku* (解毒; detoxification, anti-poison), 解 is read with its *kan-on* reading instead of its more common *go-on* reading, *kai*. Exceptions are common—情報 (*jōhō*; information), for example, is *go-kan*. 牛肉 (*gyū-niku*; beef) and 羊肉 (*yō-niku*; mutton) have *on-on* readings, but 豚肉 (*buta-niku*; pork) and 鶏肉 (*tori-niku*; poultry) have *kun-on* readings. Examples of fully *kun'yomi* compounds include 手紙 (*tegami*; letter), 日傘 (*higasa*; parasol), and the infamous 神風 (*[kamikaze](/wiki/Kamikaze "Kamikaze")*; divine wind). Some *kun'yomi* compounds have non-inflective *okurigana*, such as 唐揚げ (*karaage*; Chinese-style fried chicken) and 折り紙 (*[origami](/wiki/Origami "Origami")*); many can also be written with the *okurigana* omitted.

Kanji in isolation are typically read using their *kun'yomi*; exceptions include the *on'yomi* 愛 (*ai*; love), 禅 (*[Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen")*), and 点 (*ten*; mark, dot). Most of these *on'yomi* cases involve kanji that have no *kun'yomi*. For kanji with multiple common isolated readings, such as 金, which may be read as *kin* (gold) or *kane* (money, metal), only context can determine the intended reading.

The isolated kanji versus compound distinction gives words for similar concepts completely different pronunciations. Alone, 北 (north) and 東 (east) use the *kun'yomi* *kita* and *higashi*, but 北東 (northeast), uses the *on'yomi* *hokutō*. Inconsistencies also occur between compounds; 生 is read as *sei* in 先生 (*sensei*; teacher) but as *shō* in 一生 (*isshō*; one's whole life) (both *on'yomi*).

Multiple readings have given rise to a number of [homographs](/wiki/Homograph "Homograph"), in some cases having different meanings depending on how they are read. One example is 上手, which can be read in three different ways: *jōzu* (skilled), *uwate* (upper part), or *kamite* ([stage left/house right](/wiki/Blocking_(stage)#Stage_directions "Blocking (stage)")). In addition, 上手い has the reading *umai* (skilled). More subtly, 明日 has three different readings, all meaning "tomorrow": *ashita* (casual), *asu* (polite), and *myōnichi* (formal).

Conversely, some terms are homophonous but not homographic, and thus ambiguous in speech but not in writing. To remedy this, alternate readings may be used for confusable words. For example, 私立 (privately established, esp. school) and 市立 (municipal) are both normally pronounced *shi-ritsu*; in speech these may be distinguished by the alternative pronunciations *watakushi-ritsu* and *ichi-ritsu*. More informally, in legal jargon 前文 (preamble) and 全文 (full text) are both pronounced *zen-bun*, so 前文 may be pronounced *mae-bun* for clarity, as in "Have you memorized the preamble [not 'whole text'] of the constitution?". As in these examples, this is primarily done using a *kun'yomi* for one character in a normally *on'yomi* term.

#### Legalese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Legalese")]

Certain words take different readings depending on whether the context concerns legal matters or not. For example:

| Word | Common reading | Legalese reading |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 懈怠 ("negligence")[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) | *ketai* | *kaitai* |
| 競売 ("auction")[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) | *kyōbai* | *keibai* |
| 兄弟姉妹 ("siblings") | *kyōdai shimai* | *keitei shimai* |
| 境界 ("metes and bounds") | *kyōkai* | *keikai* |
| 競落 ("acquisition at an auction")[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) | *kyōraku* | *keiraku* |
| 遺言 ("will")[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) | *yuigon* | *igon* |
| 図画 ("imagery")[[39]](#cite_note-39) | *zuga* | *toga*[[a]](#cite_note-40) |

### Ambiguous readings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ambiguous readings")]

In some instances where even context cannot easily provide clarity for [homophones](/wiki/Homophone "Homophone"), alternative readings or mixed readings can be used instead of regular readings to avoid ambiguity. For example:

| Ambiguous reading | Disambiguated readings |
| --- | --- |
| *baishun* | *baishun* (売春; "selling sex", on) *kaishun* (買春; "buying sex", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41) |
| *itoko* | *jūkeitei* (従兄弟; "male cousin", on) *jūshimai* (従姉妹; "female cousin", on)  *jūkei* (従兄; "older male cousin", on)  *jūshi* (従姉; "older female cousin", on)  *jūtei* (従弟; "younger male cousin", on)  *jūmai* (従妹; "younger female cousin", on) |
| *jiten* | *kotobaten* (辞典; "word dictionary", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41) *kototen* (事典; "encyclopedia", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38)  *mojiten* (字典; "character dictionary", irregular, from *moji* (文字; "character"))[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41) |
| *kagaku* | *kagaku* (科学; "science", on) *bakegaku* (化学; "chemistry", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) |
| *karyō* | *ayamachiryō* (過料; "administrative fine", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) *togaryō* (科料; "misdemeanor fine", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) |
| *kōshin* | *Kinoesaru* (甲申; "[Greater-Wood](/wiki/Heavenly_Stems "Heavenly Stems")-[Monkey](/wiki/Earthly_Branches "Earthly Branches") year", kun) *Kinoetatsu* (甲辰; "Greater-Wood-Dragon year", kun)  *Kanoesaru* (庚申; "Greater-Fire-Monkey year", kun)  *Kanoetatsu* (庚辰; "Greater-Fire-Dragon year", kun) |
| *Shin* | *Hatashin* (秦; "[Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)")", irregular, from the alternative reading *Hata* used as a family name)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) *Susumushin* (晋; "[Jin](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(266%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (266–420)")", irregular, from the alternative reading *Susumu* used as a personal name)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) |
| *shiritsu* | *ichiritsu* (市立; "municipal", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) *watakushiritsu* (私立; "private", yutō)[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) |

There are also cases where the words are technically heterophones, but they have similar meanings and pronunciations, therefore liable to mishearing and misunderstanding.

| Word with an alternative reading | Word that may be confused with |
| --- | --- |
| *gishu* (技手; "assistant engineer", on), alternatively *gite*, jūbako[[40]](#cite_note-KJE-41)[[38]](#cite_note-DJR-38) | *gishi* (技師; "engineer", on) |
| *shuchō* (首長; "chief", on), alternatively *kubichō*, yutō[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) | *shichō* (市長; "mayor", on) |

### Place names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Place names")]

Several famous place names, including [those of Japan](/wiki/Names_of_Japan "Names of Japan") itself (日本 *Nihon* or sometimes *Nippon*), those of some cities such as [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") (東京 *Tōkyō*) and [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") (京都 *Kyōto*), and those of the main islands [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu") (本州 *Honshū*), [Kyushu](/wiki/Kyushu "Kyushu") (九州 *Kyūshū*), [Shikoku](/wiki/Shikoku "Shikoku") (四国 *Shikoku*), and [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido") (北海道 *Hokkaidō*) are read with *on'yomi*; however, the majority of Japanese place names are read with *kun'yomi*: 大阪 *Ōsaka*, 青森 *[Aomori](/wiki/Aomori_Prefecture "Aomori Prefecture")*, 箱根 *[Hakone](/wiki/Hakone "Hakone")*. Names often use characters and readings that are not in common use outside of names. When characters are used as abbreviations of place names, their reading may not match that in the original. The [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka") (大阪) and [Kobe](/wiki/Kobe "Kobe") (神戸) baseball team, the [Hanshin](/wiki/Hanshin_Tigers "Hanshin Tigers") (阪神) Tigers, take their name from the *on'yomi* of the second kanji of *Ōsaka* and the first of *Kōbe*. The name of the [Keisei](/wiki/Keisei_Narita_Airport_Line "Keisei Narita Airport Line") (京成) railway line—linking Tokyo (東京) and [Narita](/wiki/Narita,_Chiba "Narita, Chiba") (成田)—is formed similarly, although the reading of 京 from 東京 is *kei*, despite *kyō* already being an *on'yomi* in the word *Tōkyō*.

[Japanese family names](/wiki/Japanese_family_name "Japanese family name") are also usually read with *kun'yomi*: 山田 *Yamada*, 田中 *Tanaka*, 鈴木 *Suzuki*. Japanese [given names](/wiki/Given_name "Given name") often have very irregular readings. Although they are not typically considered *jūbako* or *yutō*, they often contain mixtures of *kun'yomi*, *on'yomi* and *nanori*, such as 大助 *Daisuke* [*on-kun*], 夏美 *Natsumi* [*kun-on*]. Being chosen at the discretion of the parents, the readings of given names do not follow any set rules, and it is impossible to know with certainty how to read a person's name without independent verification. Parents can be quite creative, and rumors abound of children called 地球 *Āsu* ("Earth") and 天使 *Enjeru* ("Angel"); neither are common names, and have normal readings *chikyū* and *tenshi* respectively. Some common Japanese names can be written in multiple ways, e.g., Akira can be written as 亮, 彰, 明, 顕, 章, 聴, 光, 晶, 晄, 彬, 昶, 了, 秋良, 明楽, 日日日, 亜紀良, 安喜良 and many other characters and kanji combinations not listed,[[43]](#cite_note-44) Satoshi can be written as 聡, 哲, 哲史, 悟, 佐登史, 暁, 訓, 哲士, 哲司, 敏, 諭, 智, 佐登司, 總, 里史, 三十四, 了, 智詞, etc.,[[44]](#cite_note-45) and Haruka can be written as 遥, 春香, 晴香, 遥香, 春果, 晴夏, 春賀, 春佳, and several other possibilities.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Common patterns do exist, however, allowing experienced readers to make a good guess for most names. To alleviate any confusion on how to pronounce the names of other Japanese people, most official Japanese documents require Japanese to write their names in both *kana* and kanji.[[37]](#cite_note-How_do_Japanese_names_work-37)

Chinese place names and [Chinese personal names](/wiki/Chinese_personal_name "Chinese personal name") appearing in Japanese texts, if spelled in kanji, are almost invariably read with *on'yomi*. Especially for older and well-known names, the resulting Japanese pronunciation may differ widely from that used by modern Chinese speakers. For example, [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong")'s name is pronounced as *Mō Takutō* (毛沢東) in Japanese, and the name of the legendary Monkey King, [Sun Wukong](/wiki/Sun_Wukong "Sun Wukong"), is pronounced *[Son Gokū](/wiki/Son_Goku_(Journey_to_the_West) "Son Goku (Journey to the West)")* (孫悟空) in Japanese.

Today, Chinese names that are not well known in Japan are often spelled in *[katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")* instead, in a form much more closely approximating the native Chinese pronunciation. Alternatively, they may be written in kanji with *katakana* *furigana*. Many such cities have names that come from non-[Chinese languages](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") like [Mongolian](/wiki/Mongolian_language "Mongolian language") or [Manchu](/wiki/Manchu_language "Manchu language"). Examples of such not-well-known Chinese names include:

| English name | Japanese name | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *Rōmaji* | *Katakana* | Kanji |
| Harbin | *Harubin* | ハルビン | 哈爾浜 |
| Ürümqi | *Urumuchi* | ウルムチ | 烏魯木斉 |
| Qiqihar | *Chichiharu* | チチハル | 斉斉哈爾 |
| Lhasa | *Rasa* | ラサ | 拉薩 |

Internationally renowned Chinese-named cities tend to imitate the older English pronunciations of their names, regardless of the kanji's *on'yomi* or the Mandarin or Cantonese pronunciation, and can be written in either *katakana* or kanji. Examples include:

| English name | Mandarin name ([pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin")) | Shanghainese name ([Wugniu](/wiki/Romanization_of_Wu_Chinese "Romanization of Wu Chinese")) | Hokkien name ([Tâi-lô](/wiki/T%C3%A2i-u%C3%A2n_L%C3%B4-m%C3%A1-j%C4%AB_Phing-im_Hong-%C3%A0n "Tâi-uân Lô-má-jī Phing-im Hong-àn")) | Cantonese name ([Yale](/wiki/Yale_romanization_of_Cantonese "Yale romanization of Cantonese")) | Japanese name | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Kanji | *Katakana* | *Rōmaji* |
| Hong Kong | *Xiānggǎng* | *shian-kaon* | *Hiong-káng* / *Hiang-káng* | *Hēung Góng* | 香港 | ホンコン | *Honkon* |
| Macao/Macau | *Àomén* | *au-men* | *Ò-mn̂g* / *Ò-muî* / *Ò-bûn* | *Ou Mún* / *Ou Mùhn* | 澳門 | マカオ | *Makao* |
| Shanghai | *Shànghǎi* | *zaon-he* | *Siōng-hái* / *Siǒng-hái* / *Siāng-hái* | *Seuhng Hói* | 上海 | シャンハイ | *Shanhai* |
| Beijing/Peking | *Běijīng* | *poq-cin* | *Pak-kiann* | *Bāk Gīng* | 北京 | ペキン | *Pekin* |
| Nanjing/Nanking | *Nánjīng* | *noe-cin* | *Lâm-kiann* | *Nàahm Gīng* | 南京 | ナンキン | *Nankin* |
| Taipei | *Táiběi* | *de-poq* | *Tâi-pak* | *Tòih Bāk* | 台北 | タイペイ / タイホク | *Taipei* / *Taihoku* |
| Kaohsiung | *Gāoxióng* / *Dǎgǒu* | *kau-yon* / *tan-keu* | *Ko-hiông* / *Tá-káu* / *Tánn-káu* | *Gōu Hùhng* / *Dá Gáu* | 高雄 / 打狗 | カオシュン / タカオ | *Kaoshun* / *Takao* |

Notes:

* Guangzhou, the city, is pronounced *Kōshū*, while Guangdong, its province, is pronounced *Kanton*, not *\*Kōtō* (in this case, opting for a *tō-on* reading rather than the usual *kan-on* reading).
* Hangzhou (expected *Kōshū*) is often pronounced *Kuishū* to disambiguate with Guangzhou.
* Kaohsiung was originally pronounced *Takao* (or similar) in [Hokkien](/wiki/Taiwanese_Hokkien "Taiwanese Hokkien") and Japanese. It received this written [name](/wiki/Kaohsiung#Etymology_and_names "Kaohsiung") (kanji/Chinese) from Japanese, and later its spoken Mandarin name from the corresponding characters. The English name "Kaohsiung" derived from its Mandarin pronunciation. Today it is pronounced either カオシュン or タカオ in Japanese.
* Taipei is generally pronounced たいほく in Japanese.

In some cases the same kanji can appear in a given word with different readings. Normally this occurs when a character is duplicated and the reading of the second character has voicing (*[rendaku](/wiki/Rendaku "Rendaku")*), as in 人人 *hito-bito* "people" (more often written with the [iteration mark](/wiki/Iteration_mark "Iteration mark") as 人々), but in rare cases the readings can be unrelated, as in *tobi-haneru* (跳び跳ねる; "hop around", more often written 飛び跳ねる).

### Pronunciation assistance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Pronunciation assistance")]

Because of the ambiguities involved, kanji sometimes have their pronunciation for the given context spelled out in [ruby characters](/wiki/Ruby_character "Ruby character") known as *[furigana](/wiki/Furigana "Furigana")*, (small *[kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")* written above or to the right of the character, e.g. 振仮名（ふりがな）) or *kumimoji* (small *kana* written in-line after the character). This is especially true in texts for children or foreign learners. It is also used in [newspapers](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") and *[manga](/wiki/Manga "Manga")* for rare or unusual readings, or for situations like the first time a character's name is given, and for characters not included in the officially recognized set of [essential kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji"). Works of fiction sometimes use *furigana* to create new "words" by giving normal kanji non-standard readings, or to attach a foreign word rendered in *katakana* as the reading for a kanji or kanji compound of the same or similar meaning.

### Spelling words

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Spelling words")]

Conversely, specifying a given kanji, or spelling out a kanji word—whether the pronunciation is known or not—can be complicated, due to the fact that there is not a commonly used standard way to refer to individual kanji (one does not refer to "kanji #237"), and that a given reading does not map to a single kanji—indeed there are many homophonous *words*, not simply individual characters, particularly for *kango* (with *on'yomi*). It is easiest to write the word out—either on paper or tracing it in the air—or look it up (given the pronunciation) in a dictionary, particularly an electronic dictionary; when this is not possible, such as when speaking over the phone or writing implements are not available (and tracing in air is too complicated), various techniques can be used. These include giving *kun'yomi* for characters—these are often unique—using a well-known word with the same character (and preferably the same pronunciation and meaning), and describing the character via its components. For example, one may explain how to spell the word *kōshinryō* (香辛料; spice) via the words *kao-ri* (香り; fragrance), *kara-i* (辛い; spicy), and *in-ryō* (飲料; beverage)—the first two use the *kun'yomi*, the third is a well-known compound—saying "*kaori*, *karai*, *ryō* as in *inryō*."

### Dictionaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Dictionaries")]

In dictionaries, both words and individual characters have readings glossed, via various conventions. Native words and Sino-Japanese vocabulary are glossed in *hiragana* (for both *kun* and *on* readings), while borrowings (*gairaigo*)—including modern borrowings from Chinese—are glossed in *katakana*; this is the standard writing convention also used in *furigana*. By contrast, readings for individual characters are conventionally written in *katakana* for *on* readings, and *hiragana* for *kun* readings. Kun readings may further have a separator to indicate which characters are *okurigana*, and which are considered readings of the character itself. For example, in the entry for 食, the reading corresponding to the basic verb *eat* (食べる, *taberu*) may be written as た.べる (*ta.beru*), to indicate that *ta* is the reading of the character itself. Further, [kanji dictionaries](/wiki/Kanji_dictionary "Kanji dictionary") often list compounds including irregular readings of a kanji.

## Local developments and divergences from Chinese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Local developments and divergences from Chinese")]

Since kanji are essentially Chinese *[hanzi](/wiki/Hanzi "Hanzi")* used to write Japanese, the majority of characters used in modern Japanese still retain their Chinese meaning, physical resemblance with some of their modern [traditional Chinese characters](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters") counterparts, and a degree of similarity with [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") pronunciation imported to Japan from the 5th to 9th centuries.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Nevertheless, after centuries of development, there is a notable number of kanji used in modern Japanese which have different meaning from *hanzi* used in modern Chinese. Such differences are the result of:

* the use of characters created in Japan,
* characters that have been given different meanings in Japanese, and
* post-[World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") simplifications (*[shinjitai](/wiki/Shinjitai "Shinjitai")*) of the character.

Likewise, the process of [character simplification](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters") in [mainland China](/wiki/Mainland_China "Mainland China") since the 1950s has resulted in the fact that Japanese speakers who have not studied Chinese may not recognize some simplified characters.

### *Kokuji*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Kokuji")]

Main article: [Kokuji](/wiki/Kokuji "Kokuji")

In addition to unique Japanese renditions of existing Chinese characters, there also exist kanji that were invented in Japan; these may be referred to as *kokuji* ([国字](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%9B%BD%E5%AD%97 "ja:国字"); national characters) or *wasei kanji* (和製漢字; Japanese-made kanji). They are primarily formed by combining existing components in unique ways, as is typical for Chinese characters. The *Jōyō* list contains about 9 *kokuji*, of which the most commonly used is [働](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%83%8D#Japanese "wikt:働") (*dō*; work) used in the fundamental verb [働く](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%83%8D%E3%81%8F#Japanese "wikt:働く") (*hataraku*; to work). It is formed from the ['person' radical](/wiki/Radical_9 "Radical 9") 亻 plus 動 (movement). Some *kokuji*, including 働, have entered the Chinese language.

The term *kokuji* may also refer to Chinese characters coined in other (non-Chinese) countries; the corresponding phenomenon in Korea is called *[gukja](/wiki/Gukja "Gukja")* ([Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language"): 국자; [Hanja](/wiki/Hanja "Hanja"): 國字; national characters); there are however far fewer Korean-coined characters than Japanese-coined ones. [Other languages](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts#Adaptations_for_other_languages "Chinese family of scripts") using the [Chinese family of scripts](/wiki/Chinese_family_of_scripts "Chinese family of scripts") sometimes have far more extensive systems of native characters, most significantly Vietnamese *[chữ Nôm](/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m "Chữ Nôm")*, which comprises over 20,000 characters used throughout traditional Vietnamese writing, and [Zhuang](/wiki/Zhuang_languages "Zhuang languages") [sawndip](/wiki/Sawndip "Sawndip"), which comprises over 10,000 characters, which are still in use.

### *Kokkun*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Kokkun")]

In addition to *kokuji*, there are kanji that have been given meanings in Japanese that are different from their original Chinese meanings. These are not considered *kokuji* but are instead called *kok‌[kun](/wiki/Kun%27yomi "Kun'yomi")* (国訓) and include characters such as the following:

| Char. | Japanese | | Chinese | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Reading | Meaning | Pinyin | Meaning |
| 藤 | *fuji* | [wisteria](/wiki/Wisteria "Wisteria") | *téng* | rattan, cane, vine |
| 沖 | *oki* | [offing](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/offing "wikt:offing"), offshore | *chōng* | rinse, minor river (Cantonese) |
| 椿 | *tsubaki* | *[Camellia japonica](/wiki/Camellia_japonica "Camellia japonica")* | *chūn* | *[Toona](/wiki/Toona "Toona")* spp. |
| 鮎 | *ayu* | [sweetfish](/wiki/Sweetfish "Sweetfish") | *nián* | [catfish](/wiki/Catfish "Catfish") (rare, usually written 鯰) |
| 咲 | *saki* | [blossom](/wiki/Blossom "Blossom") | *xiào* | [smile](/wiki/Smile "Smile") (rare, usually written 笑) |

Main article: [Chinese character classification](/wiki/Chinese_character_classification "Chinese character classification")

[Han-dynasty](/wiki/Han-dynasty "Han-dynasty") scholar [Xu Shen](/wiki/Xu_Shen "Xu Shen"), in his 2nd-century dictionary *[Shuowen Jiezi](/wiki/Shuowen_Jiezi "Shuowen Jiezi")*, classified Chinese characters into six categories ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 六書 *liùshū*, Japanese: 六書 *rikusho*). The traditional classification is still taught but is problematic and is no longer the focus of modern lexicographic practice, as some categories are not clearly defined, nor are they mutually exclusive: the first four refer to structural composition, while the last two refer to usage.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### *Shōkei moji* (象形文字)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Shōkei moji (象形文字)")]

*Shōkei* (Mandarin: *xiàngxíng*) characters are [pictographic](/wiki/Pictograph "Pictograph") sketches of the object they represent. For example, 目 is an eye, while 木 is a tree. The current forms of the characters are very different from the originals, though their representations are more clear in [oracle bone script](/wiki/Oracle_bone_script "Oracle bone script") and [seal script](/wiki/Seal_script "Seal script"). These pictographic characters make up only a small fraction of modern characters.

### *Shiji moji* (指事文字)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Shiji moji (指事文字)")]

*Shiji* (Mandarin: *zhǐshì*) characters are [ideographs](/wiki/Ideograph "Ideograph"), often called "simple ideographs" or "simple indicatives" to distinguish them and tell the difference from compound ideographs (below). They are usually simple graphically and represent an abstract concept such as 上 "up" or "above" and 下 "down" or "below". These make up a tiny fraction of modern characters.

### *Kaii moji* (会意文字)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Kaii moji (会意文字)")]

*Kaii* (Mandarin: *huìyì*) characters are compound ideographs, often called "compound indicatives", "associative compounds", or just "ideographs". These are usually a combination of pictographs that combine semantically to present an overall meaning. An example of this type is 休 (rest) from 亻 (person radical) and 木 (tree). Another is the *kokuji* 峠 (mountain pass) made from 山 (mountain), 上 (up) and 下 (down). These make up a tiny fraction of modern characters.

### *Keisei moji* (形声文字)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Keisei moji (形声文字)")]

*Keisei* (Mandarin: *xíngshēng*) characters are phono-semantic or [radical](/wiki/Radical_(Chinese_character) "Radical (Chinese character)")-phonetic compounds, sometimes called "semantic-phonetic", "semasio-phonetic", or "phonetic-ideographic" characters, are by far the largest category, making up about 90% of the characters in the standard lists; however, some of the most frequently used kanji belong to one of the three groups mentioned above, so *keisei moji* will usually make up less than 90% of the characters in a text. Typically they are made up of two components, one of which (most commonly, but by no means always, the left or top element) suggests the general category of the meaning or semantic context, and the other (most commonly the right or bottom element) approximates the pronunciation. The pronunciation relates to the original Chinese, and may now only be distantly detectable in the modern Japanese *on'yomi* of the kanji; it generally has no relation at all to *kun'yomi*. The same is true of the semantic context, which may have changed over the centuries or in the transition from Chinese to Japanese. As a result, it is a common error in folk etymology to fail to recognize a phono-semantic compound, typically instead inventing a compound-indicative explanation.

### *Tenchū moji* (転注文字)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Tenchū moji (転注文字)")]

*Tenchū* (Mandarin: *zhuǎnzhù*) characters have variously been called "derivative characters", "derivative [cognates](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate")", or translated as "mutually explanatory" or "mutually synonymous" characters; this is the most problematic of the six categories, as it is vaguely defined. It may refer to kanji where the meaning or application has become extended. For example, 楽 is used for 'music' and 'comfort, ease', with different pronunciations in Chinese reflected in the two different *on'yomi*, *gaku* "music" and *raku* "pleasure".

### *Kasha moji* (仮借文字)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Kasha moji (仮借文字)")]

*Kasha* (Mandarin: *jiǎjiè*) are [rebuses](/wiki/Rebus "Rebus"), sometimes called "phonetic loans". The etymology of the characters follows one of the patterns above, but the present-day meaning is completely unrelated to this. A character was appropriated to represent a similar-sounding word. For example, 来 in ancient Chinese was originally a pictograph for "wheat". Its syllable was homophonous with the verb meaning "to come", and the character is used for that verb as a result, without any embellishing "meaning" element attached. The character for wheat 麦, originally meant "to come", being a *keisei moji* having 'foot' at the bottom for its meaning part and "wheat" at the top for sound. The two characters swapped meaning, so today the more common word has the simpler character. This borrowing of sounds has a very long history.

See also: [Japanese typographic symbols](/wiki/Japanese_typographic_symbols "Japanese typographic symbols")

The [iteration mark](/wiki/Iteration_mark "Iteration mark") (々) is used to indicate that the preceding kanji is to be repeated, functioning similarly to a [ditto mark](/wiki/Ditto_mark "Ditto mark") in English. It is pronounced as though the kanji were written twice in a row, for example *iroiro* ([色々](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%89%B2%E3%80%85 "wikt:色々"); "various") and *tokidoki* (時々; "sometimes"). This mark also appears in personal and place names, as in the [surname](/wiki/Japanese_name "Japanese name") Sasaki (佐々木). This symbol is a simplified version of the kanji 仝, a variant of *dō* (同; "same").

Another abbreviated symbol is [ヶ](/wiki/%E3%83%B6 "ヶ"), in appearance a small *[katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana")* *ke*, but actually a simplified version of the kanji 箇, a general counter. It is pronounced *ka* when used to indicate quantity (such as 六ヶ月, *rok**ka**getsu* "six months") or *ga* if used as a genitive (as in 関ヶ原 *seki**ga**hara* "Sekigahara").

The way how these symbols may be produced on a computer depends on the operating system. In [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"), typing じおくり will reveal the symbol 々 as well as ヽ, ゝ and ゞ. To produce 〻, type おどりじ. Under Windows, typing くりかえし will reveal some of these symbols, while in Google IME, おどりじ may be used.

## Collation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Collation")]

Kanji, whose thousands of symbols defy ordering by conventions such as those used for the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), are often [collated](/wiki/Collation "Collation") using the traditional Chinese [radical-and-stroke sorting](/wiki/Radical-and-stroke_sorting "Radical-and-stroke sorting") method. In this system, common components of characters are identified; these are called [radicals](/wiki/Radical_(Chinese_character) "Radical (Chinese character)"). Characters are grouped by their primary radical, then ordered by number of pen strokes within radicals. For example, the kanji character 桜, meaning "cherry", is sorted as a ten-stroke character under the four-stroke primary radical 木 meaning "tree". When there is no obvious radical or more than one radical, convention governs which is used for collation.

Other kanji sorting methods, such as the [SKIP](/wiki/Kodansha_Kanji_Learner%27s_Dictionary#SKIP "Kodansha Kanji Learner's Dictionary") system, have been devised by various authors.

Modern general-purpose [Japanese dictionaries](/wiki/Japanese_dictionaries "Japanese dictionaries") (as opposed to specifically character dictionaries) generally collate all entries, including words written using kanji, according to their *[kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")* representations (reflecting the way they are pronounced). The *[gojūon](/wiki/Goj%C5%ABon "Gojūon")* ordering of *kana* is normally used for this purpose.

## Kanji education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kanji&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Kanji education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/3002_Kanji.svg/250px-3002_Kanji.svg.png)](/wiki/File:3002_Kanji.svg)

An image that lists most *jōyō*-kanji, according to [Halpern's KKLD indexing system](/wiki/Kodansha_Kanji_Learner%27s_Dictionary "Kodansha Kanji Learner's Dictionary"), with kyo-iku kanji color-coded by grade level

Japanese schoolchildren are expected to learn 1,026 basic kanji, the [*kyōiku* kanji](/wiki/Ky%C5%8Diku_kanji "Kyōiku kanji"), before finishing the sixth grade. The order in which these characters are learned is fixed. The *kyōiku* kanji list is a subset of a larger list, originally of 1,945 kanji and extended to 2,136 in 2010, known as the [*jōyō* kanji](/wiki/J%C5%8Dy%C5%8D_kanji "Jōyō kanji") required for the level of fluency necessary to read newspapers and literature in Japanese. This larger list of characters is to be mastered by the end of the ninth grade.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Schoolchildren learn the characters by repetition and [radical](/wiki/Radical_(Chinese_character) "Radical (Chinese character)").

Students studying Japanese as a foreign language are often required by a curriculum to acquire kanji without having first learned the vocabulary associated with them. Strategies for these learners vary from copying-based methods to [mnemonic](/wiki/Mnemonic "Mnemonic")-based methods such as those used in [James Heisig](/wiki/James_Heisig "James Heisig")'s series *[Remembering the Kanji](/wiki/Remembering_the_Kanji "Remembering the Kanji")*. Other textbooks use methods based on the [etymology](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") of the characters, such as Mathias and Habein's *The Complete Guide to Everyday Kanji* and Henshall's *A Guide to Remembering Japanese Characters*. Pictorial [mnemonics](/wiki/Mnemonic "Mnemonic"), as in the text *Kanji Pict-o-graphix* by Michael Rowley, are also seen.

The Japan Kanji Aptitude Testing Foundation provides the *[Kanji kentei](/wiki/Kanji_kentei "Kanji kentei")* (日本漢字能力検定試験 *Nihon kanji nōryoku kentei shiken*; "Test of Japanese Kanji Aptitude"), which tests the ability to read and write kanji. The highest level of the *Kanji kentei* tests about six thousand kanji.[[49]](#cite_note-50)
